/* Surgical */ I edited this section to clarify that suction curettage is preferred to sharp curettage for abortion.  I substituted suction aspiration instead of MVA as MVA is a specific case of suction aspiration; the same recommendation would apply to EVA, so I used the general case of suction aspiration.  I updated the WHO guideline citation which was republished in 2017.

/* Surgical */ I removed this sentence "curettage refers to cleaning the walls of the uterus".  I clarified what curettage is (removal of tissue).  Also, the uterus is not dirty and gynecologists do not perform D&C for any reason related to hygiene.

/* Surgical */ the author has confused terms here.  Dilation and curettage is a general term used to describe opening the cervix and removing some or all of what is inside, whether a pregnancy or other forms of tissue unrelated to a pregnancy.  MVA and EVA are forms of dilation and curettage, although sometimes dilation is not required.
/* Surgical */ As stated in my previous comment, the technique used for uterine aspiration does not determine whether or not dilation is required.

/* Surgical */ I revised this section to correct errors.  MVA and EVA do not differ n how EARLY they can be used; EVA can be used later but there is no consensus on a limit for EVAs so I did not state one.  Whether or not dilation is needed depends on the exam, not the technique of applying suction.  I cited the standard textbook on abortion which provides a summary of these procedural considerations.

This section was using decades-old data in some parts. Left in the the comparative stat as it is the most recent available, but corrected it to what it says in the study (14, not 13). People were plugging in new numbers from different time periods, which you can't do for a comparative stat like that. I added updated but separate abortion mortality and maternal mortality stats using the latest National Academies and CDC data available. Also corrected some terminologies.

Added statistic on the mortality rate ratio between abortion and carrying-to-term. The disparity is extremely large, and including it helps put the previous statement into context. The study added for reference is used by ACOG, but can be verified by comparing the sentence’s original reference’s 6 per 100,000 abortion death rate to the latest CDC pregnancy related mortality data for deaths occurring at or after birth, which about 9.8 per 100,000. (See CDC’s MAY 2019 MMWR on pregnancy deaths)
